criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the mock dataset creation using Butterpy and the prior distributions in Table 1 (inclination, period, spot lifetime, activity cycle).
The agent has read Section 2.2 completely to understand the full pre-processing pipeline including Diff-Lc computation, Diff-ACF computation, noise injection, and normalization.
The agent has read Section 2.3 completely to understand the Kepler data download procedure, quarter stitching, and the three SSL augmentations (masking, Gaussian noise, shuffle).
The agent has read Section 3.1 and Appendix B completely to understand the dual-branch model architecture, including all hyperparameters in the parameter table.
The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the SimSiam-based self-supervised contrastive pre-training framework and the stop-gradient mechanism.
The agent has read Section 4.2 to understand the 2-step training pipeline (SSL step on Kepler data followed by SBSL step on simulated data), including the training loss and optimizer settings.
The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand the five ablation model variants and the acc10p evaluation metric.
The agent has read Section 5.2.1 to understand the simulation error and observational error estimation methods, and the total error formula in Equation 6.
The agent has read Section 5.3 to understand the multi-stage filtering pipeline that produces the final catalog of 82771 Kepler stars.
The agent has read Table 3 to check the performance comparison between LightPred and the Tel-Aviv team ACF on simulated noisy and noiseless data.

The plan includes generating 50000 simulated light curves using Butterpy with the specific prior distributions from Table 1: inclination uniform in cos(i), period log-uniform (10-50 days 90%, 0-10 days 10%), spot lifetime log-uniform (1-10 period), activity cycle log-uniform (1-10 years).

The plan includes the two-step pre-processing pipeline: Savitzky-Golay filtering, Diff-Lc computation, Diff-ACF computation with interpolation, and final normalization to produce a 2-channel time series input.
The plan includes downloading the Kepler Long Cadence Data Release 25, using PDC-MAP corrected data, omitting quarters Q0-Q2 and Q17, normalizing and stitching quarters.

The plan includes the three SSL augmentations (masking 20% of time points with 20% of those replaced by random values, Gaussian noise with std 1e-4, and quarter shuffling) and random selection of two augmentations per SSL sample.
The plan includes the temporal branch architecture with 1D convolution block, max pooling, dropout, batch normalization, skip connection, bi-directional LSTM, and non-learnable self-attention block.
The plan includes the global branch architecture as a modified Astroconformer using MHSA + 1D Conv + MHSA (instead of 2 convolutions) with rotary positional embedding.

The plan includes the prediction head concatenating features from both branches, two linear layers with GELU activation and dropout, and four output values: period, inclination, period confidence, and inclination confidence.
The plan includes the SSL pre-training step using SimSiam with stop-gradient, a projection MLP (3 layers, dim 64), and a prediction MLP (2 layers, dims 32 and 64) applied to Kepler samples.
The plan includes the SBSL step with L1 loss for both period absolute deviation and period prediction, AdamW optimizer, and fixed learning rate of 5e-4.

The plan includes computing the observational error using multi-segment consistency across 7 eight-quarter segments (21 pairs per sample), fitting a normal distribution to differences, and using 1-sigma as the observational error.
The plan includes ACF and GPS implementations on the simulated test set for comparison.

The plan includes the five-step filtering pipeline for the final catalog: removing Santos et al. (2021) pulsators, replacing 2231 double-period predictions with McQuillan et al. (2014) values, removing fast rotators (period < 3 days), and removing samples with confidence < 0.86.

The code for generating simulated light curves using Butterpy with Table 1 prior distributions has been implemented, including the isotropic inclination distribution (uniform in cos(i)) rather than uniform in sin²(i).
The code for cutting the first 200 days and randomly selecting a 720-day (8-quarter) subset from simulated light curves has been implemented.

The code for Kepler noise injection using stars with w < 0.04 (6981 stars) has been implemented, including both noise addition methods: direct addition and scaled addition where std(noise) = alpha * std(sample) with alpha randomly chosen between 0.02 and 0.05.
The code for applying a Savitzky-Golay filter with window size 13 timepoints (6h) and computing the Diff-Lc (time series of consecutive differences) has been implemented.
The code for computing the Diff-ACF (ACF of the Diff-Lc with all possible lags) and filling missing values by linear interpolation has been implemented.
The code for normalizing both the Diff-ACF and Diff-Lc to zero mean and unity standard deviation, producing the final 2-channel time series input, has been implemented.

The code for loading the Kepler Long Cadence Data Release 25 using PDC-MAP correction, omitting Q0-Q2 and Q17, dividing each quarter by maximum value, normalizing to equal averages across quarters, and stitching all quarters has been implemented.

The code for SSL data augmentations has been implemented: masking 20% of time points (with 20% of masked points replaced by random values uniform between -2 and 2), Gaussian noise with std 1e-4, and quarter shuffling.
The code for randomly selecting two augmentations per SSL sample (from masking, Gaussian noise, shuffle, and identity) and creating two different views of each sample has been implemented.

The code for the temporal branch convolutional block has been implemented with kernel size 4, stride 4, channels 256, batch normalization, GELU activation, max pooling, dropout 0.35, and skip connection.
The code for the bi-directional LSTM block in the temporal branch has been implemented with 5 LSTM layers and hidden size 64.

The code for the non-learnable self-attention block in the temporal branch has been implemented, using the LSTM final cell state as queries and the last layer features as keys and values, computing attention weights via softmax.
The code for the global branch embedding 1D convolutional layer with stride 20 and encoder dimension 128, followed by batch normalization and SiLU activation, has been implemented.
The code for 5 Conformer blocks in the global branch has been implemented, each with MHSA + 1D Conv (BatchNorm + SiLU) + MHSA (not 2 convolutions), with 8 attention heads and dropout 0.3.
The code for rotary positional embedding in the global branch Conformer blocks has been implemented.

The code for the prediction head has been implemented: concatenation of temporal and global branch features, two linear layers with GELU activation and dropout, hidden size 128, and four output values (period, inclination, period confidence, inclination confidence).
The code for the SimSiam contrastive loss function (Equation 3) has been implemented as the negative cosine similarity D(p1, z2) = -(p1/||p1||) · (z2/||z2||) with stop-gradient applied to z2.
The code for the symmetric SSL loss (Equation 4) has been implemented as L = 1/2 D(p1, z2) + 1/2 D(p2, z1), with stop-gradient only through the prediction head output.
The code for the SimSiam projection MLP (3 layers, output dimension 64) and prediction MLP (2 layers, dimensions 32 and 64) for the SSL step has been implemented.
The code for the SBSL training loss (Equation 5) has been implemented as L = L1(c_hat, c) + L1(p_hat, p), where c is the absolute deviation and p is the ground truth period, both scaled to 0-1 range.
The code for defining model confidence as 1 - c_hat (where c_hat is the predicted absolute deviation) has been implemented.
The code for the AdamW optimizer with fixed learning rate of 5e-4 for SBSL training has been implemented.
The code for splitting the 50000-sample simulated dataset into 81% training, 9% validation, and 10% test sets (5000 test, 4500 validation) has been implemented.

The code for the five ablation model variants has been implemented: LightPred (full), LightPred no-diff (regular LC + ACF), Temporal Branch only, Conformer Branch only, and LightPred period only (no inclination output).
The code for the ACF baseline has been implemented on the simulated test set, with period set to zero when no peaks are found, and the acc10p/acc20p metrics computed.
The code for the GPS (Gradient Power Spectrum) baseline has been implemented on the simulated test set using the inflection point of the wavelet transform gradient as the period indicator.

The code for filtering Kepler samples to those with at least 8 consecutive quarters (130868 samples), and subsequently filtering by Teff < 7000K and removing known contaminants (delta Scuti, gamma Doradus, RR Lyrae, Giants) has been implemented.
The code for computing the simulation error by binning simulation test-set results into 1-day period bins and assigning bin-level average absolute error to Kepler predictions has been implemented.

The code for computing the observational error using 7 eight-quarter segments (21 pairs per sample), fitting a normal distribution to all pair differences, and using the 1-sigma value as the observational error has been implemented.
The code for computing the total error (Equation 6) as e_tot = e_obs / (p_hat * (1 - c_hat)) has been implemented, combining observational error, predicted period, and model confidence.
The code for computing R_var (difference between 5th and 95th flux percentiles on 90-day windows with 45-day overlap, then taking max minus min across windows) has been implemented.
The code for computing S_ph (mean standard deviation of the light curve over a window of five periods) has been implemented.
The code for the ACF comparison on multiple Kepler segments, including the observational error computation for ACF, has been implemented.

The code for the catalog filtering pipeline has been implemented: removing 1046 Santos et al. (2021) contaminants, replacing 2231 predictions with McQuillan et al. (2014) values where ACF second peak is higher and difference is a factor of 2 (+/-20%), removing 3106 fast rotators (period < 3 days by ACF/McQ14 or EB orbital period < 3 days), and removing 21173 low-confidence samples (confidence < 0.86).

The code for the eclipsing binary tidal synchronization analysis has been implemented, including computing P_orb / P_rot ratio and classifying EB types from the Villanova catalog and Lurie et al. (2017) classifications.
The code for the confidence threshold determination (0.86) using the consistency fraction per orbital-period bin procedure on eclipsing binaries has been implemented.
The SSL pre-training step has been executed successfully on the Kepler dataset (130868 samples) without errors, with training and validation loss curves showing convergence.
The SBSL supervised training step has been executed successfully on 50000 simulated samples without errors, using the SSL-initialized backbone.
The ablation study has been executed successfully, training all five model variants on the same simulated dataset and evaluating with acc10p metric.
The ACF and GPS baselines have been executed successfully on the simulated test set, producing valid period predictions for comparison.
LightPred inference has been executed successfully on the full Kepler dataset (starting from 130868 samples) without errors.
The multi-segment observational error computation and total error calculation have been executed successfully on the Kepler dataset.
The full catalog filtering pipeline has been executed successfully, producing the final catalog of 82771 stellar rotation period predictions.

The ablation study results match Table 2: LightPred achieves 78% acc10p, LightPred no-diff achieves 76%, Temporal Branch achieves 75%, Conformer Branch achieves 76%, and LightPred period-only achieves 73%.

The simulated data results match Table 3: LightPred Noiseless achieves 80% acc10p and 93% acc20p, outperforming the Tel-Aviv team ACF (75% and 90%); LightPred Noisy achieves 76% and 90%, outperforming ACF (68% and 80%).

The ACF comparison on the simulated test set matches Table 4: LightPred predicts 100% of samples with 76% acc10p and 1.60 days average error; ACF predicts only 83% with 65% acc10p and 6.86 days average error on all samples.
The GPS comparison on the simulated test set matches Table 5: LightPred outperforms GPS in all metrics, with GPS predicting only 86% of samples at 61% acc10p and 4.06 days average error.

The Kepler prediction comparison matches Table 6: LightPred predicts 100% of samples with 2.01 days average observational error; ACF predicts only 33% of samples with 2.98 days average observational error.

The self-supervised pre-training comparison matches Table 7: training with SSL achieves 78% acc10p, 92% acc20p, 1.58 days simulation error, and 2.01 days observational error; training without SSL achieves 71%, 88%, 1.80 days, and 6.77 days respectively.
The final output catalog contains 82771 stellar rotation period predictions with columns for KID, predicted period, observational error, model confidence, total error, and flag as shown in Table 8.
The execution outcomes confirm that model confidence negatively correlates with prediction absolute error (Figure 5), validating that the model learned to identify harder samples.

The execution outcomes confirm that LightPred distinguishes stellar types in eclipsing binaries (Figure 15): starspot-modulated samples (sp) with 3 < P_orb < 10 days cluster near the synchronization line (P_orb/P_rot = 1), unlike the ACF which falsely identifies ellipsoidal variations.

The execution outcomes confirm that model confidence (threshold 0.86) separates stellar types better than total error (Figure 16), with pulsators and ellipsoidal binaries having lower confidence scores.

The execution outcomes confirm that for periods < 25 days, the simulation error and observational error show a clear monotonic (approximately linear) relationship (Figure 7), validating the simulation as a good representation of real Kepler data.

The execution outcomes confirm that the total error correlates with stellar parameters including effective temperature, distance, luminosity, magnitude, S_ph, and R_var (Figure 10), with R_var showing the strongest visual correlation.
